insights: Software incidents account for 170 cases—70 more than each of the other four categories (Database, Hardware, Inquiry / Help, Network), representing a 70% relative increase over the baseline of 100, but still constituting only 25.8% of the total 670 incidents.
Four out of five incident categories are exactly balanced at 100 incidents each, indicating deliberate or systemic uniformity in category assignment or demand generation across those domains.

The near-perfect uniformity across four categories—contrasted with a modest elevation in Software—suggests no operational bottleneck, resource mismatch, or structural risk tied to category-level distribution.

With zero missing values and full coverage across all five categories, the observed distribution reflects intentional design or stable underlying processes rather than data quality issues or assignment bias.
summary: The analysis confirms no meaningful discrepancy or imbalance in incident distribution across categories: four categories (Database, Hardware, Inquiry / Help, Network) each contain exactly 100 incidents, while Software has 170—representing a modest, isolated elevation rather than a systemic skew. This near-uniform pattern implies stable demand composition or consistent categorization practices, with no evidence of operational strain, governance gaps, or service concentration that would warrant reallocation, escalation, or root-cause investigation. Given the absence of material imbalance, continued monitoring of category proportions is sufficient unless new business drivers or process changes emerge.
